coredata.dc:title: S-scheme BiOBr/Bi2WO6 with oxygen vacancies for synergistic photodegradation and hydrogen generation: Mechanisms insight and DFT calculations

coredata.dc:description: An S-type heterojunction photocatalyst Bi2WO6/BiOBr (BWO/BiOBr) with an interlaced band structure and abundant oxygen vacancies (Ov) was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and high-temperature calcination. The surface was modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to create a 0D/2D/2D structure. The resulting photocatalyst exhibited superior performance, with a degradation rate for Norfloxacin (NOR) antibiotics surpassing Bi2WO6 with oxygen vacancies (BWO-Ov) and BiOBr by 6.55 and 3.96 times, respectively. Notably, it achieved a high hydrogen generation rate of 459.49 μmol·g−1·h−1 under visible light. The enhanced carrier transfer and solar absorption were attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and S-type heterojunctions. The face-to-face 2D/2D structure provided abundant reaction sites. The influence of initial NOR concentration and photocatalyst dosage on photocatalytic activity was investigated in our study. Incorporating CQDs improved solar absorption, as confirmed by NOR degradation under various wavelengths. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis indicated the generation of reactive oxygen species. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and characterization explored charge transfer mechanisms and degradation pathways. These findings contribute to the design of efficient S-type heterogeneous photocatalysts for sustainable energy and environmental applications.

head.ce:title: S-scheme BiOBr/Bi2WO6 with oxygen vacancies for synergistic photodegradation and hydrogen generation: Mechanisms insight and DFT calculations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An S-type heterojunction photocatalyst Bi2WO6/BiOBr (BWO/BiOBr) with an interlaced band structure and abundant oxygen vacancies (Ov) was synthesized using a hydrothermal method and high-temperature calcination. The surface was modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to create a 0D/2D/2D structure. The resulting photocatalyst exhibited superior performance, with a degradation rate for Norfloxacin (NOR) antibiotics surpassing Bi2WO6 with oxygen vacancies (BWO-Ov) and BiOBr by 6.55 and 3.96 times, respectively. Notably, it achieved a high hydrogen generation rate of 459.49 μmol·g−1·h−1 under visible light. The enhanced carrier transfer and solar absorption were attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and S-type heterojunctions. The face-to-face 2D/2D structure provided abundant reaction sites. The influence of initial NOR concentration and photocatalyst dosage on photocatalytic activity was investigated in our study. Incorporating CQDs improved solar absorption, as confirmed by NOR degradation under various wavelengths. Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis indicated the generation of reactive oxygen species. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and characterization explored charge transfer mechanisms and degradation pathways. These findings contribute to the design of efficient S-type heterogeneous photocatalysts for sustainable energy and environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, there is a high demand for the use of numerous antibiotics in the treatment of diseases. Norfloxacin, a third-generation quinolone antibacterial drug, is an example of such antibiotics, exhibiting bacteriostatic properties and commonly employed in the management of enteritis and dysentery. However, the emergence of norfloxacin as an organic pollutant in wastewater has raised concerns due to its resistance to natural degradation, leading to significant environmental issues [1,2]. Consequently, the effective elimination of norfloxacin from water sources is imperative. Conventional wastewater treatment methods have limitations in degrading antibiotics in water. In this context, photocatalytic technology has emerged as a promising approach for environmental remediation, representing an advanced oxidation technology [3–7]. Additionally, the escalating global energy demand has underscored the necessity for clean and renewable energy sources. Hydrogen energy has garnered considerable attention as an efficient and environmentally friendly energy alternative, with photocatalytic hydrogen production technology gaining traction as a potentially significant method for hydrogen generation [8–10]. The activation of molecular oxygen through photocatalysis can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) for the degradation of antibiotics. The efficiency of photocatalytic processes is heavily reliant on the choice of photocatalyst, emphasizing the importance of selecting an efficient one.

Bismuth-based photocatalytic materials exhibit promises as functional materials due to their appropriate energy band structure and high stability [11–14]. These materials are primarily utilized in environmental treatment and energy conversion applications. Among them, bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) is an Aurivillius-type oxide characterized by a unique layered structure([Bi2O2]2+ layer) and Perovskite structure [WO4]2-. [11,15] The presence of a built-in electric field between the layers facilitates the acceleration of photogenerated carrier separation. However, BWO's wide band gap and limited absorption range for visible light restrict its photocatalytic activity, thereby constraining its potential applications. Consequently, the deficiencies of single-phase photocatalysts are often addressed through elemental doping, heterostructure construction, and defect introduction [16–19]. Notably, the formation of heterojunctions with internal electric fields and defect engineering can impede carrier complexation.[20–22] Nano-heterostructures can promote the photoinduced separation of electron-hole pairs and enhance the generation of ROS. Therefore, the coupling of BWO with semiconductors possessing energy level-matched properties is anticipated to be a reasonably efficient strategy [23,24]. Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr), characterized by a narrow bandgap and layered structure, exhibits excellent visible light absorption and superior photocatalytic activity [25–28]. Crucially, BiOBr nanosheets share the same [Bi2O2]2+ structure as BWO.[29] Due to lattice compatibility, BiOBr represents a potential semiconductor catalyst capable of forming heterojunctions with BWO. In previous studies, it was found that S-type heterojunctions retain the strongest redox capacity compared to other types of heterojunctions. The internal electric field formed at the S-type heterojunction can promote the separation of electrons and holes, reduce the recombination effect and increase the carrier lifetime [30–32]

Furthermore, the OVs is a widely employed defect engineering approach for enhancing the physicochemical properties and photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts [33–38]. Due to specific synthesis conditions or unstable surface structures of photocatalysts, oxygen atoms tend to migrate to arbitrary positions or even escape from the lattice. Generally, OVs are induced by high temperature and/or pressure, chemical reduction, and ionic doping, resulting in bond breakage, lattice distortion, defect state formation, and electronic compensation within the photocatalysts, thus playing a crucial role in the photocatalytic process. OVs can generate defective states between the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), serving as trapping sites and facilitating efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [39], making them widely applicable in photocatalysis and other domains. Consequently, BWO nanosheets with oxygen vacancies can be combined with BiOBr nanosheets to form a 2D/2D structure. Unlike the 0D/2D (point-to-face) and 0D/1D (point-to-face) composite structures, the 2D/2D configuration represents a face-to-face structure, which reduces carrier transport distance and enhances the abundance of reactive active sites [40–42]

In this study, we initially produced oxygen vacancy-enriched BWO photocatalysts through high-temperature calcination, followed by the creation of S-type photocatalytic heterojunctions with BiOBr loaded with CQDs using the hydrothermal method. Carbon quantum dots possess distinctive physical and chemical characteristics, including up-conversion luminescence, electron transfer efficiency, and strong light absorption capability. Incorporating CQDs with semiconductor photocatalysts offers advantages in broadening the light response range of semiconductors, enhancing the catalyst's pollutant adsorption, and facilitating the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The photocatalytic performances of BWO, BiOBr, BWO-Ov/BiOBr, and CQDs/BWO-Ov/BiOBr were evaluated through the photodegradation of NOR and generation of H2. This research aims to offer valuable insights for the development of environmentally friendly and effective photocatalysts.

BWO with oxygen vacancies was produced using a one-step synthesis method. Specifically, 2 mmol of bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O) and 1 mmol of sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2 H2O) were dissolved in 20 mL and 10 mL of ethylene glycol, respectively. The Na2WO4·2 H2O solution was then slowly added to the Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O solution with vigorous stirring for 30 minutes at room temperature. The resultant blend was subsequently placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and subjected to a reaction at 160 °C for a duration of 12 hours. Following the cooling of the autoclave to ambient temperature, the resulting product was gathered and subjected to multiple washings with ethanol and deionized water to remove any remaining residues. The resulting precipitate was then dried overnight at 60°C to obtain a pure BWO catalyst. Subsequently, the BWO was subjected to calcination at 450 °C for 4 hours to produce BWO with oxygen vacancies, denoted as BWO-Ov.

The synthesis of 2D/2D BWO-Ov/BiOBr decorated with CQDs was conducted using a hydrothermal method, as exhibited in Fig. 1a. Initially, a solution (Solution A) was prepared by dispersing 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O in 20 mL of distilled water, while another solution (Solution B) was formed by dissolving 1 mmol of KBr in 20 mL of distilled water. The BWO-Ov and CQDs were added to Solution B and stirred magnetically for 1 hour. Subsequently, Solution B was added to Solution A and stirred continuously for 1 hour. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 hours. The resulting photocatalyst powder was collected through centrifugation and stored under dry conditions. The BWO-Ov/BiOBr heterojunction was synthesized in a similar manner, except for the addition of CQDs. The mole ratios of Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O and BWO-Ov were varied at 2:10, 3:10, 4:10, and 5:10, respectively. The resulting BWO-Ov/BiOBr heterojunction and CQDs decorated BWO-Ov/BiOBr were denoted as BB-x and CBB-x, where x = 20, 30, 40, and 50, respectively.
The detailed characterization of photocatalysts were shown in Supplementing Information. All the regents were of analytical purity and shown in Table S1.

The catalysts' morphology and lattice structure were examined using various techniques including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping. Both BWO-Ov and BiOBr were observed as extremely thin nanosheets, with lengths of approximately 3 μm and 2.5 μm, respectively (Fig. S1). CBB-40 exhibited a lamellar structure composed of ultrathin two-dimensional nanosheets stacked on top of each other (Fig. 1b and c), and the TEM images showed the presence of surface-loaded CQDs (Fig. 1d). The average size of the CQDs was measured to be 13.5 nm, and their particle size distribution is depicted in Fig. S2. The nanosheets in CBB-40 had lengths of about 2–2.5 μm and a thickness of approximately 200 nm. HRTEM analysis of CBB-40 revealed noticeable lattice defects and disorder (Fig. 1e), possibly due to vacancies in the BWO lattice resulting from the absence of oxygen atoms. The lattice spacings of 0.287 nm and 0.22 nm correspond to the [102] crystal plane of BiOBr and the [113] crystal plane of BWO-Ov, respectively [43]. These crystal planes align with the sharp XRD diffraction peaks of BWO-Ov [131] and BiOBr [102], indicating the close contact between BiOBr and BWO-Ov. Additionally, EDS analysis confirmed the presence and distribution of C, O, Br, Bi and W elements in CBB-40 (Fig. 1h-m).

The x-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to examine the crystalline phases of the synthesized materials, including BiOBr, BWO, BWO-Ov, BB-40, and CBB-40 (Fig. 1f). The peaks observed in both BWO and BWO-Ov were found to align with the orthorhombic phase of BWO (PDF#39–0256) [44], indicating that the introduction of OVs did not alter the crystalline phase structure of BWO. However, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of BWO-Ov was notably reduced, possibly due to the presence of OVs causing a compressive stress state. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of BiOBr were matched with the standard card (JCPDS 73–2061) [45]. It is evident that the diffraction peaks of BWO-Ov and BiOBr were present in BB-40 and CBB-40 without any impurity peaks, suggesting the successful formation of the heterojunction and the high crystallinity of the composite structure. Notably, no peaks corresponding to CQDs were detected in the composite structures, which may be attributed to the low quantity of added CQDs. The existence of OVs was verified through ESR spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 1g. The ESR signal observed at g = 2.004 for BWO-Ov was attributed to unpaired electrons confined within OVs [46]. Additionally, the presence of OVs in BB-40 and CBB-40 was also evident, indicating the successful incorporation of OVs into the composite structure. Surface oxygen vacancies originate from lattice vibrations induced by elevated temperatures, causing oxygen atoms to detach from their positions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on the prepared samples to ascertain their chemical composition. The XPS survey spectrum of CBB-40 revealed the presence of Bi, W, Br, and O elements, consistent with the expected chemical composition of the sample ( Fig. 2a). The C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV and 286 eV were identified as C-C/CC and C-O, respectively (Fig. 2b) [47]. Notably, the peak at 288.6 eV corresponded to CO [48,49], confirming the incorporation of CQDs in the CBB-40 nanocomposite. To confirm the presence of oxygen defects, pure BWO was annealed at 400℃ for 4 h in an air-rich atmosphere to create oxygen vacancies, and a contrast sample of BWO without OV was prepared. The peaks observed at 159.2 and 164.5 eV in BWO were identified as corresponding to the Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2 orbitals of the Bi3+ ion, as depicted in Fig. 2c [50]. In contrast, BWO-OV showed a 0.2 eV shift towards the higher binding energy region, suggesting the existence of an OV defect.[51] The peaks at 529.70 and 532.10 eV in BiOBr were assigned to lattice oxygen (Bi-O) and the adsorbed oxygen on the surface, respectively (Fig. 2d) [52]. The O 1 s spectrum analysis confirmed the presence of OV in BWO-Ov, with asymmetric O 1 s peaks centered at 529.60, 531.20, and 532.50 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen species, and surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively [34,53]. The binding energy of BWO-Ov/BiOBr composite exhibited a negative shift after forming a heterojunction with BiOBr, indicating a change in electron density [54]. The observed peaks at 35.4 eV and 37.6 eV in BWO were assigned to the W 4 f7/2 and W 4 f5/2 states in [WO4]2-, respectively (Fig. 2e) [55]. The binding energy of W 4 f in BB-40 and CBB-40 was positively shifted. The BiOBr peaks at 68.0 and 68.9 eV were attributed to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d2/3, respectively (Fig. 2f) [56]. Compared with pure BiOBr, BB-40 and CBB-40 exhibited a slight negative shift, indicating an increase in electron density. The shift of binding energy identified the electron migration pathways of the obtained nanocomposites, with the negative shift of Br 3d implying that BiOBr acted as an electron acceptor in the CBB-40 composites, while the positive shift of W 4 f indicated that BWO acted as the electron donor. According to the binding energy analysis, the findings indicate that the electrons generated by light migrate from BWO to BiOBr in the CBB-40 system.

The combination of photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics and hydrogen production has the potential to simultaneously generate clean energy and address pollution issues. Consequently, our study focused on the photocatalytic production of hydrogen and degradation of NOR antibiotics. The results presented in Fig. 3a indicate that the removal efficiencies of NOR under visible light (λ≥420 nm) by BiOBr, BWO-Ov, BB-40, and CBB-40 were 20.4%, 23.99%, 52.4%, and 82.2%, respectively, within 120 min. This suggests that the incorporation of quantum dots, OVs, and S-type heterojunctions enhanced the degradation activity of NOR. Additionally, the prepared catalysts conformed to the first-order kinetics, as depicted in Fig. 3b. The reaction rate constant (k) of CBB-40 was observed to be 0.01216 min−1, which was 6.55, 3.96, and 1.32 times higher than that of BiOBr (0.00161 min−1), BWO-Ov (0.00245 min−1), BB-40 (0.00525 min−1), respectively (Fig. 3c). Furthermore, experimental results indicated that the degradation rates of CBB-40 and BB-40 were superior to those of BiOBr and BWO-Ov, attributed to the introduction of the S-scheme heterojunction, which suppressed the recombination of the photogenerated carriers. BWO-Ov and BiOBr were physically mixed together, but the catalytic activity of the mixture was lower than that of single-phase BWO-Ov and BiOBr as exhibited in Fig. S3. Possibly due to the lack of interaction and synergistic effects, physically mixed catalysts cannot exhibit higher catalytic activity than individual catalysts. For optimization, BWO-Ov/BiOBr S-scheme heterojunctions with different mole ratios (BB-20, BB-30, BB-40, and BB-50) were fabricated, and BB-40 exhibited the highest photodegradation efficiencies as demonstrated in Fig. 3d. When the doping amount of BWO-Ov is more than 40%, the degradation efficiency begins to decrease, which may be because too much BWO-Ov will block the absorption of sunlight by the catalyst, thus reducing the photodegradation efficiency. Adsorption experiments were conducted in the absence of light for 1 h, as depicted in Fig. 3e. The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved after 20 minutes of exposure to visible light. Consequently, a 30-minute period of dark reaction was introduced before initiating the photocatalytic degradation process to guarantee the full establishment of the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Furthermore, the degradation efficiencies of CBB-40 for NOR were compared under simulated solar, Ultraviolet (UV), visible, and Near infrared (NIR) light to assess the photo-utilization capabilities of the synthesized catalysts, as illustrated in Fig. 3f. The photodegradation of NOR by BB-40 and CBB-40 under simulated solar, UV, visible, and NIR irradiation surpassed that of BWO-Ov, indicating the exceptional light absorption capacity of the prepared catalysts. The photodegradation rates of CBB-40 under NIR light were found to be 3.56 and 5.05 times higher than those of BB-40 and BWO-Ov, respectively. Evidently, the introduction of CQDs expanded the range of wavelengths absorbed and enhanced the utilization of sunlight, leading to a notable increase in the degradation efficiency of NOR. Notably, the introduction of OVs as sites for trapping electrons facilitated localized electron enrichment, thereby enhancing the activation of oxygen molecules and facilitating the dissociation of excitons to produce ROS more efficiently. These results indicate that the combined impact of oxygen vacancies and heterojunctions is more powerful than their individual effects, underscoring their significant contribution to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of BWO (Fig. S4). Moreover, the hydrogen production of the CBB-40 photocatalyst demonstrated the highest rate ( Fig. 4a), nearly seven times that of the original BWO-Ov. Notably, under 350 nm visible light irradiation, the calculated apparent quantum yield (AQY) of CBB-40 was 1.05% (Fig. 4b). The hydrogen evolution rates of BiOBr, BWO-Ov, BB-40, CBB-20, CBB-30, CBB-40, and CBB-50 were 48.83, 63, 112.23, 268.33, 338.17, 459.49, and 369.03 μmol·g−1·h−1, respectively (Fig. 4c).

In consideration of practical implications, the photostability of CBB-40 with the most active heterostructure was assessed under identical degradation conditions, and the findings are depicted in Fig. S5. Following each photodegradation experiment, the photocatalysts underwent centrifugation and were rinsed with deionized water. The retrieved catalyst was subsequently subjected to overnight drying at 60 °C. The results obtained indicated a slight decrease in the photoactivity of CBB-40 (from 86.3% to 70.7%) after four cycling experiments, likely attributable to some loss of the photocatalyst. However, after four cycles, the H2 generation of CBB-40 remained highly stable, as illustrated in Fig. 4d. The empirical evidence suggests that the CBB-40 composite exhibits exceptional reusability. To further substantiate its stability, the XRD spectra of the utilized photocatalyst CBB-40 were analyzed. The outcomes (Fig. S6) revealed no alteration in the crystal structure, signifying that the prepared CBB-40 possesses superior stability and recyclability.

To determine the optimal reaction conditions, different amounts of catalysts and different concentrations of antibiotic solutions were selected for comparative experiments. As shown in Fig. 5a, under the same reaction conditions, the lower the concentration of NOR solution, the higher the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. This may be because excessive concentration can block the transmission of light. The amount of catalyst also has a significant impact on the photocatalytic activity, as shown in Fig. 5b, with the catalyst dose increasing from 0.1 g·L−1 to 0.4 g·L−1, the CBB-40 exhibited higher photocatalytic efficiency. However, as the catalyst dosage increases to 0.5 g·L−1, the degradation efficiency of NOR begins to decrease. This may be due to excessive catalysts covering each other, blocking the active sites of the reaction, thereby affecting photocatalytic activity.

The effect of pH on NOR degradation was also investigated. It is known that NOR has two differentiation constants (pKa1=6.34, pKa2=8.75), resulting in different forms of existence (NOR+, NOR ± or neutral NOR0 and NOR-) under different pH conditions, as shown in Fig. 5c. To investigate the electrostatic interaction between catalysts and antibiotics, the zeta potential under different pH conditions were tested. As shown in Fig. 5d, the zero charge point of CBB-40 is 6.45. When pH < 6.45, the net charge on the surface of CBB-40 is positive, while NOR mainly exists in the form of cations. The electrostatic repulsion between protonated NOR and positively charged CBB-40 hinders their contact, leading to a decrease in degradation efficiency. In addition, acidic conditions may lead to the aggregation of photocatalysts. When the pH value is greater than 8.75, there is a repulsive force between deprotonated NOR and negatively charged CBB-40, which is not conducive to absorption and photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 5d, under neutral conditions, CBB-40 has the highest degradation efficiency for NOR.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of NOR were obtained by DFT calculations ( Fig. 6a and b) to probe the active sites of NOR that are susceptible to attack. The HOMO of NOR is mainly located in the piperazinyl ring, which suggests susceptibility to ring-opening reactions. Additionally, the regional selectivity of photocatalytic active substances was further predicted using Fukui indices. Atoms with higher f-, f+, and f0 values respectively indicate that they are active sites more susceptible to electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attacks. As shown in Fig. 6c, active sites with higher f0 values have been labeled in red, which represents that they are more susceptible to free radical attacks. Based on the above analysis, the possible degradation pathways as well as the degradation products were predicted. As shown in Fig. 6d, the main processes of NOR degradation include ring opening (TP1, TP2, and TP3), decarboxylation (TP5), defluorination (TP4, TP6), oxidation and cleavage (TP7, TP8, and TP9). The above results indicate that antibiotics were gradually decomposed and oxidized.

The rapid separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers play a crucial role in enhancing the efficacy of photocatalysis. To further investigate the effects of CQDs and OVs on charge separation and transfer efficiency, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra, transient photocurrent response, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were studied. The photocurrent intensity of CBB-40 and BB-40 was found to be 12.5 times and 5 times that of the original BWO-Ov, respectively ( Fig. 7a). EIS spectroscopy primarily serves to elucidate the extent of charge transfer resistance at the interface between solid and liquid. [12,50,57]As depicted in Fig. 7b, among all the samples, CBB-40 exhibited the smallest semicircle diameter, indicating the lowest interface resistance. A decrease in PL intensity corresponds to a reduced recombination rate of light-driven h+/e- pairs. The PL intensity followed the order CBB-40 < BB-40 < BWO-Ov, suggesting that CBB-40 experiences the lowest photogenerated carrier recombination rate, as depicted in Fig. 7c. In order to detect the up-conversion characteristics of the prepared CQDs, PL test was carried out on the CQDs solution after dialysis. As shown in Fig. S7, CQDs was excited with light of 600–900 nm wavelength, and the emission peak of CQDs mainly concentrated in the region of 500–650 nm. The main peak wavelength and peak intensity of the emission spectrum varied with the wavelength of the excitation source, that is, the up-conversion emission spectrum obviously depended on the wavelength of the excitation source. These findings collectively demonstrate that the h+/e- pair charge separation efficiency of CBB-40 is the highest, underscoring the effective role of CQDs and OVs in inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

The assessment of the photocatalyst's light absorption capacity is crucial for determining the catalytic efficacy of the resulting catalyst. This was conducted using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As depicted in Fig. 7d, the absorption edge of BiOBr is approximately 420 nm, whereas that of BWO is around 450 nm. In comparison to the original BWO, the absorption edge of BWO-OV is shifted towards longer wavelengths, leading to a significant increase in light absorption within the 400–800 nm range, indicating an enhancement in light harvesting capability due to the presence of OVs. The light absorption of BB-40 is redshifted as a result of the coupling of the photocatalyst. Furthermore, the introduction of OVs and CQDs resulted in significantly higher light absorption intensities in the CBB-40 composites compared to BiOBr, BWO, and BB-40, owing to the combined effect of Ov modification and CQDs introduction. The band gap (Eg) of the prepared photocatalysts was determined based on UV–vis DRS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 7e, the calculated band gaps of BWO, BiOBr, BWO-Ov, BB-40, and CBB-40 were 2.72 eV, 2.77 eV, 2.64 eV, 2.62 eV, and 2.58 eV, respectively. It is evident that the introduction of oxygen vacancies and CQDs resulted in a reduction in the band gap of the composites, aligning with the findings from the light absorption analysis. Furthermore, the Mott-Schottky (M-S) analysis was conducted to investigate the conduction band potential and specific band structure of the synthesized photocatalysts. As depicted in Fig. S8, the flat band potential (Efb) values for BWO-Ov and BiOBr are −0.15 and 1.31 eV relative to Ag/AgCl (0.05 and 1.51 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (vs. NHE)). The M-S curves of BWO-Ov demonstrate positive slopes, while those of BiOBr exhibit negative slopes, indicating that BWO-Ov and BiOBr are categorized as n-type and p-type semiconductors, respectively. Generally, the conduction band potential (ECB) of n-type semiconductors is approximately 0.1 eV lower than the Efb, whereas the valence band potential (EVB) of p-type semiconductors is approximately 0.1 eV higher than the Efb. Consequently, the ECB of BWO-Ov and the EVB of BiOBr are roughly determined to be −0.05 eV and 1.61 eV, respectively. Similarly, the EVB of BWO-Ov and the ECB of BiOBr are calculated to be 2.59 eV and −1.16 eV, respectively. Additionally, XPS valence band spectra were utilized to further ascertain the VB potentials of BWO-Ov and BiOBr (Fig. 7f). The measured VB potentials of BWO-Ov (2.60 eV) and BiOBr (1.60 eV) align with those obtained from the aforementioned analyses. Consequently, the energy level structures of BiOBr and BWO-Ov were determined, as illustrated in Fig. 9c.

The electronic structures and the density of states (DOS) for BWO, BWO-Ov, and BiOBr were obtained through DFT calculations based on the corresponding structure models (see Fig. 8g-i). The DOS for BWO, BWO-Ov, and BiOBr are illustrated in Fig. 8a-c. The valence band maximum (VBM) of BiOBr is a result of the hybridization of O 2p and Br 3p orbitals, while the conduction band minimum (CBM) is primarily composed of Bi 6p orbitals [58]. In the case of BWO, VBM and CBM are predominantly influenced by O 2p and Bi 6p-W 5d orbitals [12,59]. Notably, compared to pure BWO, oxygen-vacancy-rich BWO exhibits increased DOS, suggesting that the presence of OVs promotes electron transfer and leads to higher concentrations of charge carriers. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 8d-f，the VBM and CBM of BiOBr are situated at different points in the Brillouin zone, indicating that BiOBr is an indirect band gap semiconductor [60], whereas BWO is a direct band gap semiconductor [61]. The calculated band gaps for BWO, BWO-Ov, and BiOBr were found to be 2.40 eV, 2.28 eV, and 2.37 eV, respectively.

Based on the determined positions of the band edges, the heterojunction may exhibit an II type electron transfer pathway (Fig. 9d), leading to the accumulation of e- and h+ on the CB of BWO-Ov and the VB of BiOBr, respectively. However, the CB potential of BWO-Ov is insufficiently negative to facilitate the reduction of O2 to produce ·O2 -, and the VB potential of BiOBr is not sufficiently positive to oxidize H2O to produce ·OH. This finding contradicts the results obtained from ESR analysis. To further investigate the electron transfer mechanism of CBB-40, the work function of the semiconductor photocatalysts was determined through DFT calculations, which is crucial for elucidating the charge transfer pathway. Fig. 9a and b illustrate the electrical potentials of BWO-Ov and BiOBr, with the work function of BWO-Ov measured at 5.76 eV and the work function of BiOBr at 4.66 eV. A higher work function signifies the transfer of electrons [62], indicating that in this case, electrons are transferred from BWO-Ov to BiOBr until reaching the equilibrium of the Fermi level. Based on the aforementioned data analysis, a more plausible S-type heterojunction is proposed (Fig. 9e). At the interface of the S-heterojunction, electrons will accumulate at the BWO-Ov layer and be depleted at the BiOBr layer, resulting in an internal electric field. Consequently, under visible light illumination, the ineffective h+ and e- are effectively separated after the formation of the S-scheme heterojunction, retaining carriers with strong redox ability. Additionally, the band edges of BiOBr and BWO-Ov bend upward and downward, respectively, indicating the transfer of electrons, which aligns with the XPS results. The formation of the S-type heterojunction not only facilitates carrier transfer but also preserves strong redox ability, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 9

In situ XPS analysis was performed to investigate the charge transfer at the heterojunction under optical radiation. In CBB-40, the W 4 f binding energy underwent a significant positive shift after light exposure, and the electron density on the W atom decreased under light exposure ( Fig. 10a). Under light irradiation at 365 nm, the Br 3d peak presents a negative shift and the electron density increases (Fig. 10b). These visible binding energy changes provide direct evidence to confirm electron transfer from BWO-Ov to BiOBr.[63,64]

ESR experiments were conducted to confirm the presence of OH and ·O2 - species. As depicted in Fig. 10c and d, the signals of DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH were observed in CBB-40 under visible light irradiation, thus validating the significant contributions of ·OH and ·O2 -. Furthermore, to explore the impact of potential reactive species on the photodegradation of NOR, triethanolamine (TEOA), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) were employed as scavengers to capture h+, ·OH and ·O2 -, respectively. The radical-trapping experiments (Fig. 10e) revealed that the inclusion of IPA and BQ impeded the degradation of NOR, indicating the crucial involvement of ·OH and ·O2 - in NOR degradation. Conversely, the addition of TEOA had minimal influence on the efficiency of NOR degradation, suggesting its negligible role in this photodegradation process. This consistent with the ESR result.

Based on the aforementioned findings, the photocatalytic process of CBB-40 was elucidated, and the reaction mechanism is depicted in Fig. 11. Initially, BiOBr and BWO-Ov produce electrons and holes when exposed to visible light. The e- on the CB of BWO-Ov and the h+ on the VB of BiOBr recombine within the internal electric field. Subsequently, the remaining holes on the VB of BWO-Ov react with H2O to form •OH radicals, while the remaining e- on the CB of BiOBr react with oxygen to produce •O2 - radicals. As the generated electrons are excited and transferred to the CB position of BiOBr, they reduce H2O to H2. Simultaneously, the S-type heterojunction retains the useful electrons and holes with strong redox capability by promoting the recombination of nonessential carriers. The constructed 2D/2D nanosheets structure is crucial for the degradation process, as it possesses ample reactive active sites to facilitate the separation of e-/h+ pairs. Furthermore, the specific "face-to-face" structure reduces the distance for charge transfer and facilitates the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). CQDs serve as electronic bridges to facilitate electron transfer between BWO-Ov and BiOBr, while their up-conversion effect enhances light absorption and utilization [28,65,66]. The introduction of OVs also plays a vital role in the overall process. Initially, the generation of oxygen vacancies creates defect energy levels and reduces the band gap of BWO-Ov to enhance light utilization. Additionally, oxygen defects can serve as electron traps to accommodate photogenerated electrons and promote carrier separation. OVs can also act as reactive active sites to promote the adsorption and degradation of NOR. Importantly, the introduction of OVs leads to redistribution, which positively influences the photocatalytic activity of BWO-Ov. Ultimately, NOR is mineralized into small molecule substances such as CO2 and H2O by the generated ROS. The total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency reached 37.2%, as demonstrated in Fig. 10f.

In summary, the synthesized CQDs/BWO-Ov/BiOBr photocatalyst, featuring an S-type heterojunction and interlaced band structure, demonstrated superior performance in photocatalytic applications. The incorporation of CQDs facilitated efficient carrier transfer and enhanced solar absorption, resulting in improved degradation efficiency for NOR antibiotics. The face-to-face 2D/2D structure provided abundant active sites for photocatalytic reactions. The CQDs/BWO-Ov/BiOBr catalyst also exhibited high hydrogen generation (459.49 μmol·g−1·h−1) under visible light. The findings highlight the synergistic effect of CQDs and S-type heterojunctions, offering promising prospects for the development of efficient photocatalysts. The electron transfer process and reaction mechanism of the heterojunction were examined using ESR and DFT calculations. This study contributes to advancing the understanding of the potential applications of vacancy engineering in environmental remediation and solar energy utilization.
